trajectory_summary: This is a short reschedule call that achieves its minimum outcome — a new time on the calendar — but leaves the appointment structurally exposed. Sean self-identified as unreliable twice, and Brandon's response was administrative rather than anchoring. The call neither built energy nor lost it; it processed a transaction without reinforcing the commitment.
outcome_dispute: The appointment is booked, but the v1 tag (unknown) should be read as 'conditional booking / fragile sit.' Sean explicitly said he forgets things, his calendar was not in front of him when he agreed, and the button-up protocol was not completed. Per the schema's setter compensation model, a sit that no-shows does not pay. This appointment carries meaningful no-show risk that the transcript evidence supports.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: Yeah. Yeah. Text me again. Text me on Monday. Just so yeah. I don't forget because I'll be forgetting shit.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Sean flagged his own unreliability twice and Brandon's confirmation structure was passive throughout — no verbal handshake, no one-hour block, no computer/no-driving anchor. This is a direct no-show risk on a customer who already no-showed once. Per the schema's setter compensation model, a sit that no-shows does not pay. The button-up protocol is the paycheck-protection tool Brandon did not use.

key_moments.quote: just reaching back out here about that solar evaluation for your home. Looks like we did not get a chance to meet with you to go over that solar evaluation, but, I would love to reschedule that appointment.
key_moments.stewart_read: Brandon's opener is purely logistical — reschedule the missed sit. There is no re-anchor of Sean's original motivation for signing up. On a reschedule call, the rep's first job is to remind the customer why they were interested in the first place before asking for a new time. Brandon skips that entirely and goes straight to calendar coordination. Strategic read: possibly Brandon is keeping it brief to avoid re-opening objections. Reactive read: Brandon simply defaulted to the administrative task. The reactive read holds — there is no evidence of a deliberate strategy to defer re-anchoring.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. I forgot all about that shit yesterday.
key_moments.stewart_read: Sean's first line is a reliability signal, not just an apology. A rep reading this correctly would recognize that the button-up protocol — verbal handshake, one-hour block, computer/no-driving anchor — is especially critical here. Brandon responds with 'That's alright,' which is socially smooth but strategically inert. The forgetfulness signal is absorbed, not converted.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. You sure you won't forget?
key_moments.stewart_read: Brandon's line is the right instinct — he recognizes the reliability gap — but the execution is a mild jab rather than a structural fix. 'You sure you won't forget?' puts the burden on Sean to reassure Brandon, which Sean cannot do (his calendar isn't in front of him). The move that would actually reduce no-show risk is the button-up protocol: lock the time, state the one-hour block, get the verbal handshake. Brandon identified the problem and then didn't solve it.

key_moments.quote: Well, I usually get off work at, like, four, but then I go to the gym after, which is why, yesterday, I said 06:30.
key_moments.stewart_read: Sean is explaining his schedule in detail — gym after work, 6:30 as the window. This is the natural moment to deploy the no-driving/at-computer anchor from the button-up protocol. Sean is telling Brandon exactly where he'll be and what he'll be doing at appointment time. Brandon does not use this to confirm 'you'll be home, off the gym floor, able to sit at a computer.' The information is present; the anchor is not made.
